summary: The dominant narrative that Steve Ballmer was a failed CEO who held Microsoft back until Satya Nadella saved the company is fundamentally wrong. While Ballmer faced intense criticism for missing mobile and other trends, he actually oversaw massive revenue growth (from $14-22B to $83B), made crucial long-term bets that define Microsoft today (Azure, Office 365, Bing, Xbox), and built the enterprise sales machine that powers the company. He inherited a company hamstrung by antitrust constraints and brutal internal politics, then cleared both obstacles while subsidizing multi-billion dollar moonshots that critics derided but which positioned Microsoft for decades of success.
key_points: Revenue grew from $14-22B to $83B under Ballmer, with $27B in profit when he left
Ballmer launched Azure (worth ~$1T), Office 365, and built world's most effective enterprise sales org
Even Ballmer's 'failures' like Bing would be worth $240B as standalone companies
He inherited a company constrained by antitrust and internal politics, cleared both for his successor
Microsoft made successful transitions from boxed software to cloud and from software to hardware (Xbox, Surface) that most companies fail at
money_quotes: Even Bing, widely considered a failure, on last reported revenue and current P/E ratio, would be 12th most valuable tech company in the world, between Tencent and ASML.
Microsoft salespeople noted that they would still usually be able to close the sale of Microsoft's paid product even when competing against a Google that was giving their product away.

Much like Gary Bernhardt's talk, which was panned because he made the problem statement and solution so obvious that people didn't realize they'd learned something non-trivial, Ballmer set up Microsoft for future success so effectively that it's easy to criticize him for being a bum because his successor is so successful.

If we look at the broader history of the tech industry, Microsoft has had sustained strong execution from its founding in 1975 until today, a nearly fifty year run, a run that's arguably been unmatched in the tech industry.
Basically, no matter what people think Ballmer is doing, they say it's wrong and that he should do the opposite.
key_insight: Ballmer's real achievement was making massive, heavily-criticized long-term bets that set up trillion-dollar business units and clearing political obstacles so thoroughly that his successor's success made him look incompetent by comparison.
